title: Yeasound Tries to Prove That Behind-the-Ear Hearing Aids Can Still Feature Innovative Tech
gist: Yeasound, a hearing-aid subsidiary of 25-year-old Chinese telecom hardware maker Yealink, launched the AI-powered RIC800 behind-the-ear hearing aid at $599, scoring a mediocre 6/10 in WIRED's review due to frequent Bluetooth/app disconnects and overstated battery life, despite genuinely strong noise cancellation and bass response.

insights.claim: Yeasound is a new hearing-aid brand that is actually a subsidiary of Yealink, a Chinese telecom hardware producer with 25 years of experience making headsets and phone equipment.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: Signals a telecom-hardware incumbent using its acoustics/Bluetooth manufacturing base to enter the fast-growing OTC hearing aid market — a vertical-integration entry pattern worth watching for other adjacent hardware players.
insights.quote: The company is relatively new, but it's actually a subsidiary of Yealink, a Chinese telecom producer that's been making headsets and phone hardware for 25 years.

insights.claim: Yeasound's higher-end RIC800 model ($699, $599 with reviewer's discount) adds AI-powered noise reduction, automatic speech-focusing, and Android support, differentiating it from the Apple-only RIC700.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: Cross-platform (Android + iOS) support at a sub-$600 price point positions Yeasound competitively against pricier incumbents in the OTC hearing aid market that often skew iOS-first.

insights.claim: Despite the bulk of hearing-aid market innovation shifting toward in-ear models, Yeasound demonstrates there is still room for innovation in the traditional behind-the-ear (BTE) form factor.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: Suggests the BTE hearing-aid segment isn't fully commoditized/legacy, offering a still-viable entry point for feature differentiation rather than pure in-ear disruption.
insights.quote: While the bulk of innovation in the over-the-counter hearing aid market revolves around more modern in-ear models, a new brand called Yeasound is proving there's still some life left in the traditional behind-the-ear (BTE) hearing aid space.

insights.claim: The reviewer found the iYeasound companion app prone to frequent, sometimes random Bluetooth disconnects, often requiring three or four resets per day to restore functionality.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: Persistent app/connectivity reliability issues are a recurring failure mode in AI-hardware wearables generally, and a key reason hardware-capable new entrants still struggle against established players with mature software stacks.
insights.quote: What was at first a minor nuisance eventually became the most annoying part of my testing, often requiring three or four resets each day.

insights.claim: Yeasound claimed 31 hours of battery life for the RIC800, but the reviewer's actual testing maxed out at 18 hours, a significant downgrade from the marketed spec.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: A roughly 42% gap between marketed and real-world battery life is a material spec-inflation red flag for consumer trust and product review credibility going forward.
insights.quote: Yeasound also overstated its battery life by a large margin, claiming 31 hours of running time on a charge. My testing however maxed out at 18 hours

insights.claim: The Adaptive AI mode strips away manual fine-tuning controls, and the reviewer found his own manual adjustments in General mode outperformed the AI algorithm, especially at maximum noise cancellation.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: A concrete case where user-tunable manual controls beat an AI 'adaptive' mode — a caution against assuming AI automation is automatically superior to expert manual configuration in consumer hardware.
insights.quote: This sounds liberating, but I preferred using the General mode much of the time, with my own fine-tuning proving more effective than the algorithm's

insights.claim: The RIC800's onboard hearing test/audiogram completes in about five minutes versus 10+ minutes for typical competitors by eliminating unnecessary pauses between frequency tests.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: A 2x speedup in onboarding friction (audiogram test time) is a meaningful UX differentiator that could drive adoption among users deterred by lengthy OTC hearing-aid setup processes.

insights.claim: The RIC800 was designed without metal charging contacts, instead recharging like an electric toothbrush, which Yeasound says reduces rusting-related durability issues and skin allergies.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: A specific hardware design choice (contactless induction charging) that addresses a known long-term failure mode (corrosion) in hearing aids — a durability differentiator competitors could adopt.
insights.quote: the RIC800 aids are designed without metal charging contacts and recharge instead more like an electric toothbrush

insights.claim: Yeasound equipped the RIC800 with surprisingly solid bass response for Bluetooth music streaming, overcoming the typically tinny sound most BTE hearing aids produce during music playback.
insights.speaker: Christopher Null
insights.so_what: Bluetooth streaming quality is becoming a competitive battleground for hearing aids as they increasingly double as everyday earbuds — a feature convergence trend in the category.
insights.quote: Music over typical BTE hearing aids is often tinny to the point of being unlistenable, but Yeasound has equipped the RIC800 with a surprisingly solid bass response that overcomes these issues.
